definition: Cross-validation is a resampling evaluation protocol that repeatedly trains on subsets of labeled data and scores on held-out folds, producing a more stable estimate of out-of-sample performance than a single random split.
layman: Instead of one practice exam, you rotate through several: each time you study most of the material and get quizzed on a different slice you did not study. Averaging those quiz scores gives a fairer picture of how well you really know the subject.
content:
## Why cross-validation exists

A single **train/validation/test split** (see Train/Val/Test Splits) is enough for one-shot reporting, but it can be **noisy** when data is limited or class balance varies by chance. **Cross-validation (CV)** reuses the same labeled dataset through multiple disjoint train/eval rotations so the reported score reflects performance across many partitions—not luck from one shuffle.

This topic is the **canonical home** for **k-fold CV**, **stratified k-fold**, and related **evaluation protocols** (repeated k-fold, leave-one-out). It focuses on **how to measure** generalization reliably during model comparison and reporting.

For CV as a **regularization or overfitting mitigation** technique (early stopping, nested tuning loops, variance reduction in small data), see **Cross-Validation under Overfitting Mitigation**—same mechanics, different primary goal.

### k-fold cross-validation

Given n labeled samples and integer **k** (commonly 5 or 10):

1. Partition data into **k** disjoint folds of roughly equal size.
2. For each fold **i = 1…k**:
   - **Train** on the union of all folds except i.
   - **Evaluate** on fold i (held out).
3. Record metric **mᵢ** on each held-out fold.
4. Report **mean(m)** as the CV score and often **std(m)** as a stability diagnostic.

Each sample appears in exactly **one** validation fold and **k − 1** training folds. No sample is scored on the model that trained on it.

```
Fold 1: [val | train train train train]
Fold 2: [train | val train train train]
...
Fold k: [train train ... train | val]
CV score = mean(metric on each val slice)
```

### Stratified k-fold

For **classification** with imbalanced classes, random folds may leave rare classes out of a validation slice. **Stratified k-fold** assigns points to folds so **each fold preserves the global class proportion** (as closely as partition sizes allow).

Use stratified CV when reporting accuracy, F1, ROC-AUC, or any metric sensitive to class prevalence. For **regression**, stratification is optional; practitioners sometimes bin continuous targets for stratified splits when the label distribution is skewed.

### Leave-one-out CV (LOOCV)

**LOOCV** is k-fold with **k = n**: each fold is a single point. It maximizes training data per iteration but is expensive (n refits) and can yield high variance in the aggregate score when n is small.

### Repeated k-fold

**Repeated k-fold** runs the same k-fold partition with different random shuffles (or different fold assignments) **R** times and averages all k×R fold scores. This reduces sensitivity to one particular fold boundary—useful when n is modest.

### Nested cross-validation (evaluation vs tuning)

When you **compare models or hyperparameters** using CV scores, you risk **optimistic bias** if the same CV loop both picks the winner and reports its score. **Nested CV** wraps an **inner** CV loop (tuning) inside an **outer** CV loop (unbiased performance estimate). This topic treats nested CV as part of the **evaluation protocol**; mitigation-focused workflows that rely on inner loops for regularization are covered under Overfitting Mitigation.

### What CV estimates

CV produces an estimate of **expected out-of-sample error** on data drawn from the same distribution as your sample. Fold-to-fold **spread** in scores relates to **variance** in the learning process (see Variance in the bias–variance tradeoff)—high std(m) across folds signals an unstable model or brittle split geometry.

CV does **not** replace a **locked test set** when you need a final sign-off after many experiments. Common practice:

- **CV on train (+ val)** for model and hyperparameter comparison.
- **One held-out test set** touched once for final reporting (Train/Val/Test Splits).

### Preprocessing and leakage discipline

Every CV iteration must fit **preprocessing** (scaling, imputation, feature selection) on **training folds only**, then transform validation folds. Fitting a scaler on all data before splitting leaks validation statistics into training—same rule as hold-out splits.

### Relationship to other topics

| Topic | Role relative to CV |
| --- | --- |
| **Train/Val/Test Splits** | Hold-out partitions; CV often runs on train+val while test stays locked. |
| **Variance** | High fold-to-fold score variance indicates unstable generalization. |
| **Ridge and Lasso** | Regularized linear models are often compared via CV over penalty strength α. |
| **Random Forests** | Tree ensembles with many hyperparameters (depth, `max_features`) are commonly ranked by stratified CV AUC or RMSE. |
| **Overfitting Mitigation → CV** | Same resampling machinery used primarily to reduce overfitting and tune with nested loops. |

### What this topic is not

- **Defining generalization** — the performance concept being estimated (Generalization).
- **Data leakage patterns** — duplicate users, target encoding on full data (Data Leakage).
- **Mitigation-first CV workflows** — early stopping, model selection as variance reduction (Overfitting Mitigation).

The core idea here: **rotate held-out folds, average scores, and report spread—so model comparison reflects protocol, not one lucky split.**
keyPoints: k-fold CV trains on k−1 folds and evaluates on the held-out fold; the mean fold score estimates out-of-sample performance.
Stratified k-fold preserves class proportions in each fold—essential for imbalanced classification metrics.
Fit preprocessing inside each CV fold on training data only; never leak validation statistics into fit.
Use CV for model comparison during development; reserve a locked test set for final sign-off when possible.
High variance across fold scores signals instability—connects to the variance term in bias–variance analysis.
For mitigation-focused nested tuning and small-data workflows, see Overfitting Mitigation → Cross-Validation.
